items.title: Piano vs guitar: Which instrument suits you best?
items.editorNotes: Rewritten for clarity and readability while preserving factual content and links.
items.excerptBefore: Have you ever been in a heated debate about the superior instrument — the piano vs. the guitar? Well, you're not alone. Let’s dive in!
items.excerptAfter: Explore the unique characteristics, strengths, and challenges of the piano and guitar to find the instrument that suits you best.
items.seoTitleBefore: Piano vs guitar: Which instrument suits you best?
items.seoTitleAfter: Piano vs Guitar: Which Instrument Suits You Best?
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Have you ever been in a heated debate about the superior instrument — the piano vs. the guitar? Well, you're not alone. Let’s dive in!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover the differences between piano and guitar, including portability, expressiveness, price, and more, to help you choose the right instrument for your…
items.beforeBody:
Have you ever been in a heated debate about the superior instrument - the piano vs the guitar? Well, you're not alone. It's a question that has sparked countless discussions among musicians and music enthusiasts alike. But here's the thing - it's not about superiority. It's about understanding each instrument's unique characteristics, strengths, and challenges. And that's precisely what we're going to dive into today.

As a classically trained pianist who has transitioned to the guitar, I've had the privilege of experiencing the best of both worlds. So, whether you're a beginner trying to decide which instrument to start with, or an experienced musician considering learning a new instrument, this post is for you. Let's get started!

Artistic and practical considerations

One of the biggest reasons you may choose one instrument over another is how well it fits into your lifestyle and aligns with your desires for artistic expression. We’ll explore some factors including portability, expressiveness, price, and depth of sounds, to help you find the right instrument.

Portability

The guitar has a clear advantage in terms of portability. Its compact size and lightweight design make it an ideal companion for musicians on the go. This extends to the home as well. Unlike pianos, which require dedicated space, a guitar can comfortably fit into any corner of your room. Speaking of portability, a wearable metronome like the Soundbrenner Core 2 is the perfect accompaniment to a guitar or piano, helping you keep time and stay in rhythm, wherever your music takes you.

Expressiveness

While both the piano and guitar offer a wide range of expressive possibilities, the guitar provides a unique avenue for emotional expression. Techniques such as vibrato, pitch bends, and slides can add a new dimension to your music, allowing you to impart more feeling into your performances. While common on the guitar, these techniques are not as easily replicated on the piano. The tactile nature of the guitar, where you can physically bend the strings or slide your fingers along the fretboard, offers a direct connection to the sound that many musicians find appealing.

Price

When it comes to price, both the piano and the guitar can be pretty similar. Both instruments offer a wide range of options, from budget-friendly models to high-end ones, catering to different skill levels and budgets. Whether you're a beginner looking for an affordable start or a professional seeking a top-tier instrument, the piano and the guitar have something to offer.

Depth of sounds

In terms of the variety of sounds, digital pianos and synthesizers take the lead. With the press of a button, you can access a vast array of sounds, from different piano types to various other instruments and sound effects. This versatility can add a fun and creative dimension to your music-making. On the other hand, while electric guitars can also produce a range of sounds, they often require additional equipment like effects pedals. The variety of sounds is also generally more limited compared to digital pianos.

Collectability

One unique aspect of guitar culture is the hobby of collecting. Many guitarists enjoy collecting, investing in, and selling vintage or otherwise collectable guitars. This hobby not only allows them to own a piece of music history but also provides an opportunity to decorate their homes with beautiful instruments. Collecting guitars can also be seen as an investment, as some vintage and rare models can appreciate over time.

Of course, there are collectable and vintage pianos as well, but these are typical of the grand and baby grand variety, and thus due to their size and cost are less common to be collected by the average hobbyist.

Piano vs guitar for beginners

If you're a beginner looking to embark on your musical journey, choosing between the piano and the guitar can be a daunting task. Both instruments have their unique challenges and advantages. Let's break it down:

Physical requirements

Starting with the guitar, one of the first challenges beginners face is building finger strength and developing calluses. The steel strings of a guitar can be tough on the fingers, and it takes time for your fingertips to get used to the pressure. On the other hand, the piano doesn't require you to build up finger strength or calluses in the same way. However, it does require you to develop precision and speed, especially when playing complex pieces that involve both hands.

Hand synchronization

Both piano and guitar require synchronization between your hands. For guitar, this involves strumming or picking with one hand while fretting notes with the other. This can be more intuitive for beginners as both hands are often playing the same rhythm. On the piano, however, each hand often plays a different part, which can be challenging for beginners. You might be playing a steady rhythm with your left hand while your right-hand plays a melody, or vice versa. This requires a higher level of coordination and independence between the hands.

Theory and memorization

When it comes to theory and memorization, the guitar has a bit of an edge. Once you learn the basic chord shapes, you can move them up and down the neck to play different chords. This shape-based approach simplifies the learning process and makes it easier to play songs early on. The piano, on the other hand, requires more pure memorization. The shapes you play will change with different chords, and you'll need to learn to read music on the grand staff, including the treble and bass clefs.

Sheet music vs guitar tabs

Another point to consider is the difference between sheet music and guitar tabs. While both instruments have their forms of written music, guitar tabs are generally considered easier for beginners. They show exactly where to place your fingers on the fretboard, without requiring you to memorize the order of notes like sheet music does. This makes it easier to start playing songs quickly on the guitar.

Practising rhythm

Regardless of the instrument you choose, practising rhythm is crucial. A great tool to help with this is the BeatBuddy, a drum machine that makes practising rhythm fun and engaging. It allows you to play along with various drum beats, helping you develop a solid sense of timing and rhythm.

Accessibility

Both the piano and the guitar are highly accessible instruments for beginners. Countless resources are available online, including video tutorials, interactive apps, and online music lessons. These resources can help you learn at your own pace and explore different styles of music.

Piano vs guitar as tools for music composition

When it comes to music composition, both the piano and the guitar offer unique advantages. The choice between the two often depends on the composer's personal preference, the style of music, and the specific needs of the composition.

Range and versatility

The piano has a significant advantage in terms of range. With 88 keys, the piano can play basslines, treble-based melodies, and chord progressions that sit in the middle of a mix, offering a broad canvas for composers to paint their musical ideas. This range allows for complex harmonies and the ability to play multiple parts simultaneously, making the piano an excellent tool for composing and arranging music.

In many cases, digital pianos and synthesizers are equipped with built-in drum modes that also allow you to play your drum beats.

Ease of recording and MIDI pattern creation

Recording your musical ideas is an essential part of the composition process. Both the piano and the guitar can be recorded using a variety of methods, from traditional microphones to direct input into a digital audio workstation (DAW).

While both instruments can also be used as MIDI controllers, the piano's layout and outputs make it particularly well-suited for creating MIDI patterns. The linear arrangement of keys allows for a visual representation of music theory concepts, making it easier to compose and experiment with different melodies, harmonies, and rhythms.

For those looking for a DAW-less solution for recording tracks and songs, the Aeros Loop Studio by Singular Sound is a fantastic option. This device allows you to easily record, layer, and loop your ideas without the need for a computer, creating full songs on the fly.

Expressiveness and sound variety

About the above, each instrument has pros and cons.

The guitar offers unique expressive possibilities that can inspire creativity. Techniques such as vibrato, pitch bends, and slides can add a unique flavour to your compositions. This expressiveness makes it popular with singers/songwriters who often choose acoustic guitars as their instruments of choice.

On the other hand, digital pianos and synthesizers further expand the piano's versatility in composition. With the press of a button, you can switch from a grand piano sound to strings, brass, synths, and more. This variety of sounds can be a great source of inspiration and can add different textures and colours to your compositions. This makes synthesizers and pianos a common choice for hip-hop and electronic musicians.

Conclusion

Choosing between piano vs guitar is a very personal decision. Each instrument offers unique advantages and challenges, and the best choice often depends on your preferences, lifestyle, and musical goals.

With its portability, expressiveness, and culture of collecting, the guitar can be an excellent choice for those who value convenience, emotional expression, and the joy of owning unique instruments. Its ease of learning and the simplicity of guitar tabs can also make it an appealing choice for beginners.

On the other hand, with its wide range, sound versatility, and visual layout of keys, the piano offers a comprehensive music composition platform. Its suitability for creating MIDI patterns and the ease of switching between different sounds on digital pianos and synthesizers make it a powerful tool for composers and producers.

Ultimately, don’t forget that it doesn’t have to be piano vs guitar. You can choose both and experience the pros of each instrument. Whatever you decide, pick an instrument, start playing, and let the music guide you!

items.title: How to develop a solid rhythm foundation and rock the beat
5 ways you can get gigs as a musician in 2025
items.editorNotes: Revised for clarity and readability while maintaining factual content.
items.excerptBefore: Need help getting gigs as a musician? Here are our top 5 tips to help you fill up those calendar slots with gigs galore!
items.excerptAfter: Discover five effective strategies to secure more gigs as a musician in 2025, from perfecting your craft to networking and collaborating.
items.seoTitleAfter: 5 Ways to Get Gigs as a Musician in 2025
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Need help getting gigs as a musician? Here are our top 5 tips to help you fill up those calendar slots with gigs galore!
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore our top five tips for musicians looking to secure more gigs in 2025, including perfecting your craft, networking, and enhancing your EPK.
items.beforeBody:
Need help getting gigs as a musician? Here are our top 5 tips to help you fill up those calendar slots.

Band, singer, or solo musician, it seems like everyone’s wondering how to get more gigs as a musician for a multitude of reasons. Blame it on changing preferences, easier access to recorded tunes, or just about everyone picking up a musical hobby during the pandemic lockdowns. 

Let alone making it big; it is getting harder even to book the occasional local gigs as a musician. So we've compiled a list of our top tips to help musicians like you break through the dry patch and make it start rainin’ gigs!

How to get gigs as a musician in 2025

Dedicated practice, persistence, and these top three tips will surely help you book gigs no matter what music niche you're passionate about!

(Every artist wonders how to get gigs as a musician at some point in their musical journey)

1. Perfect that tune!

Perfecting your craft is the best way to stay inspired and guarantee more gigs. So use that free time to help you get as close to perfection as possible. 

Here at SoundBrenner, we believe in the artists that make music come alive. So, we've designed the Core 2 Vibrating Metronome to help make your practices more efficient. 

Like you, this smartwatch and wearable metronome is a musical all-rounder with features including a stopwatch, tuner, and DB meter. So invest in one now to improve rhythm and timekeeping, and stay on schedule when that calendar gets jam-packed with performances!

2. Strengthen your networking game.

Networking with other artists is a great way to get gigs as a musician.

You can expand your network by participating in musical events, and professional events, and by joining online forums such as Facebook groups.

Another great way to gain free publicity and get gigs as a musician (fingers crossed!) is by participating in local competitions and sing-offs. 

Tip: Check out this guide tolearn more about networking.

3. Collaborate to get exposure and gigs.

Consider going out of your comfort zone and joining hands with artists to create mutually beneficial partnerships. Not only will it increase your exposure to a new audience, but it's also a surefire way to gain experience and network. 

Your local radio station can be a collaboration opportunity with great potential to help you book events and gigs. You never know when someone from the audience might need a musician like you for a gig or event!

Tip: Check out this guide to learn to collaborate with other artists.

4. Join a tour

If gigs are hard to come by in your locality, consider part-time touring with a fellow band or even solo. Use your network and the internet to browse and line up gigs nearby where your talent might be more appreciated. 

You can even replace a musician that had to drop out of a planned tour and gain touring experience with an established musician or band.

Tip: Check out this guide on how to open for a touring band.

5. Fire up your EPK

Build up your EPK by adding a list of versatile and exciting gigs you've performed. Show it off to local businesses to increase your chances of landing gigs.

Knowing when, where, and how to showcase your EPK is just as important. 

Keep your social media accounts updated. Display demo videos and your latest work on as many platforms as possible to showcase your EPK. 

Tip: Check out this guide on how to create a solid EPK.
items.afterBody:
Need help getting gigs as a musician? Here are our top 5 tips to help you fill up those calendar slots.

Whether you're in a band, a singer, or a solo musician, many are wondering how to secure more gigs for various reasons. Factors such as changing preferences, easier access to recorded music, and the rise of musical hobbies during the pandemic have contributed to this challenge.

Even booking local gigs has become increasingly difficult. To assist musicians like you in overcoming this dry spell, we've compiled our top tips to help you start landing more gigs!

How to get gigs as a musician in 2025

With dedicated practice, persistence, and these top tips, you can book gigs regardless of your music niche!

(Every artist wonders how to get gigs as a musician at some point in their musical journey)

1. Perfect that tune!

Perfecting your craft is essential for staying inspired and securing more gigs. Use your free time to strive for perfection.

At SoundBrenner, we believe in the artists who bring music to life. Our Core 2 Vibrating Metronome is designed to make your practice sessions more efficient.

This smartwatch and wearable metronome is a versatile tool featuring a stopwatch, tuner, and DB meter. Invest in one to enhance your rhythm and timekeeping, ensuring you're ready when your calendar fills up with performances!

2. Strengthen your networking game.

Networking with other artists is a valuable way to secure gigs.

Expand your network by attending musical events, participating in professional gatherings, and joining online forums like Facebook groups.

Participating in local competitions and sing-offs can also provide free publicity and help you land gigs.

Tip: Check out this guide to learn more about networking.

3. Collaborate to get exposure and gigs.

Consider stepping out of your comfort zone and collaborating with other artists to create mutually beneficial partnerships. This approach can increase your exposure to new audiences and provide valuable experience.

Your local radio station may offer collaboration opportunities that can help you book events and gigs. You never know when someone in the audience might need a musician like you!

Tip: Check out this guide to learn to collaborate with other artists.

4. Join a tour

If gigs are scarce in your area, consider part-time touring with another band or even going solo. Use your network and the internet to find gigs in locations where your talent may be more appreciated.

You might even have the chance to replace a musician who had to drop out of a planned tour, gaining valuable touring experience with an established artist or band.

Tip: Check out this guide on how to open for a touring band.

5. Fire up your EPK

Enhance your Electronic Press Kit (EPK) by including a list of versatile and exciting gigs you've performed. Show it to local businesses to boost your chances of landing gigs.

Knowing when, where, and how to showcase your EPK is equally important.

Keep your social media accounts updated. Display demo videos and your latest work across multiple platforms to effectively showcase your EPK.

Tip: Check out this guide on how to create a solid EPK.

items.title: 12 best gifts for bass players in 2025

items.excerptBefore: Looking for the best gifts for bass players, 2025? Here are 12 gears your loved ones wouldn’t be able to resist and thank you for.
items.excerptAfter: Discover the 12 best gifts for bass players in 2025, from essential gear to premium instruments, perfect for all skill levels.
items.seoTitleAfter: 12 Best Gifts for Bass Players in 2025
items.seoDescriptionBefore: Looking for the best gifts for bass players, 2025? Here are 12 gears your loved ones wouldn’t be able to resist and thank you for.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Explore our curated list of the best gifts for bass players in 2025, featuring essential gear, instruments, and accessories for all skill levels.
items.beforeBody:
Are you looking for the perfect gift for the bass players in your life? We’ve listed our favourite gears and best gifts for Our team has compiled a list of best gifts for bass players from beginners, intermediate to advanced. Check out the latest gears perfect for piano enthusiasts!

1. Strings: La Bella 760FS Deep Talkin' Bass

Imagine strings that get better as they get older - this is the set of strings for your favourite bass players. La Bella 760FS Deep Talkin' Bass is a stainless steel flat wound - it gives off classic vintage tones that won't damage your friend's expensive bridge neck. You can keep it in your case for a long time as it is packaged well with MAP Technology which ensures its freshness.

Why we love it:

Each string is hand-polished for an incredibly smooth finish

2. Bass guitar cable: GLS Audio 10

Your bass player friend will be forever grateful to you for getting this top-selling cable, GLS Audio 10. What makes this cable super durable and flexible is its OFC Insulator shield and PVC shield. It is famous for its stylish braided tweed cloth jacket, which gives it a modern appearance.

Starts at $14.49

3. Vibrating metronome: Soundbrenner Pulse

Yes, we may be cheating by displaying our own product, but we really do believe that the foundation of a great bass player is being able to play on a steady beat.

Beginners and proficient players alike need to practice with a metronome - beginners will often play really slow on complicated parts of the song and then play quickly through their favorite parts. In order to keep the same tempo - they need to use a metronome! Metronomes are among the best gifts for bass players.

Why a vibrating metronome? Feeling the beat through vibrations also gets around the problem of not hearing the metronome, especially when you’re dealing with deep tones.

Soundbrenner Pulse is the world's first vibrating metronome - this innovation will surprise your bass players.

4. Bass amplifier: Trace Elliot ELF Ultra Compact Bass Amplifier

If you have a gigging friend or if perhaps you are a bassist yourself and would like to gift yourself something a little more than $200 USD, we recommend the Elf bass AMP. This is one of the smallest yet most powerful bass amplifiers out there. It has a clean output without complicating EQ. It also comes with a Nylon carrying bag that should protect it from that gigging lifestyle.

Starts at $350 USD

5. Maintenance set: MusicNomad The Nomad Tool Set

If you want a simpler gift, we recommend MusicNomad a good cleaning tool set. All types of guitarists need this! It will help prolong the life of and preserve the tone of your current strings.

6. Guitar pick: Original Dragon's Heart

As much as people don’t want to admit it - guitar picks actually play a role in the way your stringed instrument sounds. Our favourite is Original Dragon's Heart an odd-shaped guitar pick - it claims 1000 hours of durability. It measures 2.5mm in thickness and caters to different playing styles.

Gift this to your bassist, and you’ll see a consistent, balanced attack.

7. Beginner's bass: Squier by Fender Bronco Bass

Fender’s line Squier has always been the easiest and most popular choice for younger beginners or small players - with its short 30“ scale length. It is light, sounds great and is inexpensive. You can be sure to play alternative rock and progressive metal straight out of the box. It has a slim and comfortable “C”-shaped neck profile, and a single-coil Bronco Bass pickup. You can last the entire gig carrying its thin and lightweight body. Its satin finish neck makes it easy to slide through the fret.

Why we love it:

You can play it straight out of the box!

8. Premium bass: Yamaha TRBX504 TBL

Looking for a 4-String Premium Electric Bass Guitar? Yamaha TRBX500 has a simple brief tone. Its premium mahogany body and design deliver a flexible and sheer tonal quality to allow you to sculpt the music you like. Another perfect guitar for beginners to intermediate bassists, versatile for anybody still learning different styles of music. It is definitely stylish and protected by Yamaha’s lifetime warranty.

Why we love it:

Deep, punchy tone

9. Professional bass:  Fender American Professional II Precision Bass V 3-color Sunburst with Rosewood Fingerboard

If your bassist is looking to upgrade to a more industry-standard instrument - they can turn to Fender’s precision bass line - both producers and engineers know this instrument. It already comes with a hardshell case.  One of this new it is a split-coil pickup which allows it to have enough punch and growl.

Why we love it:

3-colour sunburst and its body finish of glass polyurethane

10. Amp: Behringer Ultra-DI DI400P

DI or Direct Inject allows you to run bass directly into microphone preamplifiers and also to send signals over extended cable runs without losing volume and significant high-frequency. It’s one of the best gifts for bass players who want to ensure clean and reliable sound transmission.

One of the famous and easy choices is Radial ProDI. {what does this mean} Of course now you have more choices and if we could choose one Beringer Ultra-DI DI400P, it is very compact and the price is far below what most boxes cost, high-performance. Perfect for both stage and studio use, its black and rugged metal case will take the beating of roadshows.

11. Bass cabinet: Peavey Headliner 410 Bass Enclosure

Peavey is a brand that will always deliver pro quality. This is one of our favourite bass cabinets - although it might require a workout to carry this cab around, it has a great balanced and focused tone - that won’t falter even at extremely high volume. You have the option of 400 watts, 500 watts, and 800 watts.

12. Bass methods: Hal Leonard Bass Method - Complete Edition: Books 1, 2 And 3

Want a more practical and theoretical gift for a bassist? You might try to find other books on Bass theory, but this one stood the test of time and was very user-friendly. If you have a friend who is aspiring to be a bassist, this one will direct them towards the right direction. It’s definitely among the best gifts for bass players.

Starts at $22.48 USD
items.afterBody:
Are you searching for the perfect gift for the bass players in your life? We’ve compiled a list of the best gifts for bass players, suitable for beginners, intermediates, and advanced musicians. Check out the latest gear perfect for bass enthusiasts!

1. Strings: La Bella 760FS Deep Talkin' Bass

Imagine strings that improve as they age. The La Bella 760FS Deep Talkin' Bass strings are stainless steel flat wound, producing classic vintage tones without damaging your friend's expensive bridge neck. They are packaged with MAP Technology to ensure freshness.

Why we love it: Each string is hand-polished for an incredibly smooth finish.

2. Bass guitar cable: GLS Audio 10

Your bass player friend will appreciate this top-selling cable, the GLS Audio 10. Its OFC insulator shield and PVC shield make it durable and flexible. The stylish braided tweed cloth jacket gives it a modern appearance.

Starts at $14.49

3. Vibrating metronome: Soundbrenner Pulse

While we may be showcasing our own product, we believe that a steady beat is crucial for any bass player. Beginners often struggle with tempo, playing slowly on complex parts and quickly on their favorites. A metronome helps maintain consistent timing.

The Soundbrenner Pulse is the world's first vibrating metronome, allowing players to feel the beat through vibrations, which is especially helpful for deep tones.

4. Bass amplifier: Trace Elliot ELF Ultra Compact Bass Amplifier

If you have a gigging friend or want to treat yourself to something over $200, consider the Trace Elliot ELF. This compact yet powerful amplifier offers clean output without complicated EQ settings and comes with a nylon carrying bag for protection.

Starts at $350 USD

5. Maintenance set: MusicNomad The Nomad Tool Set

If you're looking for a simpler gift, the MusicNomad Tool Set is an excellent cleaning tool set. It's essential for all guitarists, helping to prolong the life and preserve the tone of their strings.

6. Guitar pick: Original Dragon's Heart

Guitar picks significantly influence the sound of stringed instruments. Our favorite is the Original Dragon's Heart, a uniquely shaped pick that boasts 1000 hours of durability. Its 2.5mm thickness accommodates various playing styles.

Gift this to your bassist for a consistent, balanced attack.

7. Beginner's bass: Squier by Fender Bronco Bass

Fender’s Squier Bronco Bass is a popular choice for young beginners or smaller players, featuring a short 30" scale length. It's lightweight, sounds great, and is affordable, making it perfect for alternative rock and progressive metal. The slim “C”-shaped neck profile and single-coil Bronco Bass pickup enhance playability.

Why we love it: You can play it straight out of the box!

8. Premium bass: Yamaha TRBX504 TBL

For a 4-string premium electric bass guitar, consider the Yamaha TRBX504. Its mahogany body delivers a flexible tonal quality, making it suitable for beginners to intermediate players exploring various music styles. Plus, it comes with Yamaha’s lifetime warranty.

Why we love it: Deep, punchy tone.

9. Professional bass: Fender American Professional II Precision Bass V

If your bassist is ready to upgrade to an industry-standard instrument, they should consider the Fender American Professional II Precision Bass. This model is well-known among producers and engineers and comes with a hardshell case. It features a split-coil pickup for added punch and growl.

Why we love it: The 3-color sunburst finish and glass polyurethane body finish.

10. Amp: Behringer Ultra-DI DI400P

The DI (Direct Inject) allows you to connect your bass directly to microphone preamplifiers, ensuring clean sound transmission over long cable runs without losing volume or high frequencies. The Behringer Ultra-DI DI400P is compact and affordable, making it a high-performance choice for both stage and studio use.

11. Bass cabinet: Peavey Headliner 410 Bass Enclosure

Peavey consistently delivers professional quality. This bass cabinet offers a balanced and focused tone, even at high volumes. It is available in 400 watts, 500 watts, and 800 watts options.

12. Bass methods: Hal Leonard Bass Method - Complete Edition

For a practical and theoretical gift, consider the Hal Leonard Bass Method - Complete Edition. This user-friendly book series has stood the test of time and is perfect for aspiring bassists, guiding them in the right direction.

Starts at $22.48 USD

items.title: Your ultimate guide for recording studio microphones
items.editorNotes: The article has been rewritten for clarity and readability while maintaining the original factual content.
items.excerptBefore: You want to start to record your own music, and you don’t know where to start with what microphones to get.
items.excerptAfter: A comprehensive guide to selecting the right microphones for recording music, covering types, frequency response, and sensitivity.
items.seoTitleBefore: Your ultimate guide for recording studio microphones
items.seoTitleAfter: Your Ultimate Guide to Recording Studio Microphones
items.seoDescriptionBefore: You want to start to record your own music, and you don’t know where to start with what microphones to get.
items.seoDescriptionAfter: Discover the essential information for choosing the right microphones for your music recordings. Learn about dynamic, condenser, and ribbon microphones…
items.beforeBody:
So you want to start to record your own music, but you don’t know where to start with what microphones to get?

Here are some questions to ask yourself before you begin looking for different models available.

What will you be using it for?

What is the main purpose or for what instrument will you be looking to use it? Are you a multi-instrumentalist - playing multiple instruments and mixing in your vocals? Acoustic drums, for example, are complex instruments and would require multiple different microphones. Check out our full guide on recording drums here.

Different frequency response for mics

High-frequency instruments like acoustic guitars, cymbals, and acoustic piano would need condenser mics. As for your low-mid frequency instrument, it would be best if you use a dynamic mic.

All microphones have a standard frequency range of 20Hz - 20000Hz. We will show you the frequency response of mics that you would use for a low-frequency sound like a kick or a bass guitar. Sennheiser MD421 and AKG D112 have different sound signatures. D112 is great for bass guitars as it has a boost on the low end from 50hz to 200hz. Using it for vocals can cause it to sound muddy as it lacks clarity on the high end.

 

On the other hand, you would use the Shure SM57 or Sennheiser MD421 to mic Guitar amps as it gives a boost on the high frequency to achieve clarity and attack/punch. If you are choosing microphones - frequency matters because it is how you gauge the mic’s sound signature and their frequency response. 


Looking at frequency response graphs from the brand of the microphone should help gauge the user what to use it for. 

Microphones field of sensitivity

Yes, microphone sensitivity is important! In a musician’s term, some mics are “hot” or “loud” when others are not. Audio engineers or mixers will have to adjust the “gain” of the microphone depending on its right recording level.

Direction of the microphones can also affect its sensitivity. The main three directions: cardioid, super cardioid, and hypercardioid. Cardioid microphone has a heart-shaped polar pattern, it rejects sounds coming from a 180-degree field.

A healthy vocal recording level is about -18db(decibels), different microphones will have different gain, but as long as you aim for the right level it should be fine.

What type of microphone?

There are 3 main types of microphones: Dynamic, condenser, and ribbon. In this section, we will go through the advantages and disadvantages of each, as well as how they should be used in the studio.

Dynamic

Advantages: 

  • Highly rugged and durable
  • Less expensive than other types of microphones
  • No phantom power required

Disadvantages: 

  • Not as sensitive as condenser microphones

Best used for: electric guitars(miking up an amp), drums, live sound (live concert, gig)

Dynamic microphones are the most versatile and commonly found type of microphone and can be used in a variety of different recording environments. Most dynamic microphones feature a cardioid pickup pattern, meaning that they capture sound waves that are in front of them.

Dynamic microphones are durable and rugged, so they can withstand being tossed around and dropped without getting damaged. They can also handle high sound levels without a problem, unlike condenser microphones. They also do not require phantom power, making them compatible with a wider range of devices. If you can only get one mic, a dynamic is the way to go.

Our recommendation: Shure SM57 Dynamic Instrument Microphone

The SM57 is a legendary dynamic microphone that can be found both in recording studios and in a live sound aspect. Besides being incredibly affordable, it is also one of the most versatile mics out there, and can be used for everything from snare drums to electric guitars. The SM57 sounds so good that artists like Bon Iver and Sufjan Stevens have recorded entire albums on just a single one of these. If you’ve only got the cash for one microphone, the SM57 is it.  

Condenser

Advantages: 

  • High level of detail
  • Sensitivity

Disadvantages:

  • It’s fragile, requires a better storage and handling
  • Doesn’t work well with loud noise

Best used for: Vocals, acoustic instruments, drum overheads

Condenser microphones are highly sensitive, as they require  phantom power (12v-48v) to polarize the plates and run the preamps inside them. Because of their sensitivity, condenser mics are most commonly used in studio settings where sound and acoustics can be tightly controlled. Condensers will pick up sounds that other microphones cannot, allowing for more detailed recordings.


Unfortunately, condenser microphones are fragile unlike the dynamic microphones, so you need to be careful when handling them. Additionally, condenser microphones require phantom power, so you will need to invest in an audio interface that can provide this functionality. Finally, condenser mics have a tendency to distort at high volumes, making them less suited to recording loud instruments (Unless you gain it correctly at the right decibel level).

Our recommendation: Rode NT1-A

The Rode NT1-A is a great condenser mic that offers studio-grade sound at an affordable, reasonable price point. Described by Rode as one of the world’s quietest studio condenser mics, it has virtually no background hiss like you’ll find on other condenser microphones at this price point. It even comes with a pop filter and shock mount, which means you won’t have to spend additional money on accessories.

Ribbon

Advantages: 

  • Warm sound signature
  • Highly sensitive and captures a lot of detail
  • Bidirectional polar pattern makes them useful for specific recording situations

Disadvantages:

  • Very fragile
  • Expensive
  • Not suited for those looking for a flat frequency response

Best used for: Piano, drum overheads, acoustic instruments

Our recommendation: Royer R-121 Studio Ribbon Microphone

The Royer R-121 is one of the most well-known ribbon microphones on the market. While being quite pricey. The R-121 is highly versatile and will provide amazing sound quality in a wide variety of applications, including electric guitars, drums, string instruments, and more.

 

Out of the three different microphone types, ribbon microphones are the most expensive and fragile. Because of this, they are generally much less popular than dynamic and condenser mics. Ribbon microphones are bidirectional and will pick up sounds coming from both the front and the back, while isolating noise from the sides. They are also the most sensitive type of microphone available, and are best used on acoustic instruments and strings. Ribbon mics produce a darker sound signature that can add a lot of warmth to your recordings.

Impedance

Output Impedance is usually measured by ohms - and typical microphones measure as 200
Ohms. This allows for long cable runs without sound degradation. Electric guitars have high impedance, which only allows the cable to run 30 ft.

If you are just purchasing a studio microphone, it won’t really matter that much - you’ll be fine with 200 Ohms.
items.afterBody:
So you want to start recording your own music, but you don’t know where to begin with choosing microphones?

Here are some questions to consider before exploring different models available.

What will you be using it for?

What is the main purpose, or which instrument will you be using it for? Are you a multi-instrumentalist who plays multiple instruments and mixes in vocals? For example, acoustic drums are complex instruments that require multiple microphones. Check out our full guide on recording drums here.

Different frequency response for mics

High-frequency instruments like acoustic guitars, cymbals, and acoustic pianos require condenser microphones. For low-mid frequency instruments, a dynamic microphone is preferable.

All microphones have a standard frequency range of 20Hz to 20,000Hz. For low-frequency sounds like a kick drum or bass guitar, microphones such as the Sennheiser MD421 and AKG D112 have different sound signatures. The D112 is excellent for bass guitars due to its boost on the low end from 50Hz to 200Hz, but it may sound muddy for vocals as it lacks clarity on the high end.

Conversely, the Shure SM57 or Sennheiser MD421 are ideal for miking guitar amps, as they enhance high frequencies for clarity and punch. When selecting microphones, frequency response is crucial as it helps gauge the mic’s sound signature.

Reviewing frequency response graphs from the microphone brand can help determine the best use for each model.

Microphones field of sensitivity

Microphone sensitivity is important! Some mics are considered “hot” or “loud,” while others are not. Audio engineers often adjust the microphone's gain to achieve the right recording level.

The directionality of microphones also affects sensitivity. The three main types are cardioid, super cardioid, and hypercardioid. A cardioid microphone has a heart-shaped polar pattern, rejecting sounds from a 180-degree field.

A healthy vocal recording level is around -18dB (decibels). Different microphones have varying gain levels, but aiming for the right level is essential.

What type of microphone?

There are three main types of microphones: dynamic, condenser, and ribbon. In this section, we will discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each, as well as their best uses in the studio.

Dynamic

Advantages:

  • Highly rugged and durable
  • Less expensive than other types of microphones
  • No phantom power required

Disadvantages:

  • Not as sensitive as condenser microphones

Best used for: electric guitars (miking up an amp), drums, live sound (concerts, gigs)

Dynamic microphones are versatile and commonly found in various recording environments. Most feature a cardioid pickup pattern, capturing sound waves from the front.

They are durable and can withstand rough handling, and they handle high sound levels well. Dynamic microphones do not require phantom power, making them compatible with a wider range of devices. If you can only purchase one mic, a dynamic microphone is the best choice.

Our recommendation: Shure SM57 Dynamic Instrument Microphone

The SM57 is a legendary dynamic microphone found in both recording studios and live sound settings. It is affordable and versatile, suitable for everything from snare drums to electric guitars. Artists like Bon Iver and Sufjan Stevens have recorded entire albums using just this microphone. If you can only afford one microphone, the SM57 is the one to get.

Condenser

Advantages:

  • High level of detail
  • Highly sensitive

Disadvantages:

  • Fragile, requiring careful storage and handling
  • Not suitable for loud noises

Best used for: vocals, acoustic instruments, drum overheads

Condenser microphones are highly sensitive and require phantom power (12V-48V) to operate. Due to their sensitivity, they are commonly used in controlled studio settings, capturing sounds that other microphones may miss.

However, condenser microphones are fragile compared to dynamic microphones, necessitating careful handling. They also require an audio interface that provides phantom power. Additionally, they may distort at high volumes, making them less suitable for loud instruments unless properly adjusted.

Our recommendation: Rode NT1-A

The Rode NT1-A is an excellent condenser mic that offers studio-grade sound at an affordable price. It is known as one of the quietest studio condenser mics, with virtually no background hiss. It also includes a pop filter and shock mount, eliminating the need for additional purchases.

Ribbon

Advantages:

  • Warm sound signature
  • Highly sensitive, capturing a lot of detail
  • Bidirectional polar pattern for specific recording situations

Disadvantages:

  • Very fragile
  • Expensive
  • Not suitable for those seeking a flat frequency response

Best used for: piano, drum overheads, acoustic instruments

Our recommendation: Royer R-121 Studio Ribbon Microphone

The Royer R-121 is a well-known ribbon microphone that, while pricey, is highly versatile and provides excellent sound quality for various applications, including electric guitars, drums, and string instruments.

Among the three microphone types, ribbon microphones are the most expensive and fragile, making them less popular than dynamic and condenser mics. Ribbon microphones are bidirectional, capturing sound from both the front and back while isolating noise from the sides. They are the most sensitive type of microphone available and are best suited for acoustic instruments and strings, producing a darker sound signature that adds warmth to recordings.

Impedance

Output impedance is typically measured in ohms, with most microphones measuring around 200 ohms. This allows for long cable runs without sound degradation. Electric guitars have high impedance, limiting cable runs to about 30 feet.

If you are purchasing a studio microphone, impedance will not be a significant concern; 200 ohms will suffice.
